# Confidence regions for $\vbeta$

## Distribution of $\hvbeta$

Assume

$$
\vveps \sim \Norm(\vzero,\sigma^2\mI)
$$

Recall

$$
\hvbeta = (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top\vY
$$

Since $\vY = \mX\vbeta + \vveps$,

$$
\hvbeta
= (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top(\mX\vbeta+\vveps)
= \vbeta + (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top\vveps
$$

Therefore

$$
\Ex(\hvbeta) = \vbeta
$$

and

$$
\cov(\hvbeta)
= \sigma^2(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}
$$

Thus

$$
\hvbeta \sim
\Norm\!\left(
\vbeta,\;
\sigma^2(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}
\right)
$$

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Confidence ellipsoid for $\vbeta$

## Quadratic form

From the normal distribution of $\hvbeta$,

$$
\frac{
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top
(\mX^\top\mX)
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
}{\sigma^2}
\sim \chi^2_d
$$

Replacing $\sigma^2$ with

$$
S^2=\frac{\|\hvveps\|^2}{n-d}
$$

gives

$$
\frac{
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top(\mX^\top\mX)(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
}{dS^2}
\sim F_{d,n-d}
$$

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## $(1-\alpha)$ confidence region

Therefore

$$
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top
(\mX^\top\mX)
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
\le
d S^2
F_{d,n-d,1-\alpha}
$$

defines a

$$
\class{alert}{(1-\alpha)\text{ confidence ellipsoid for }\vbeta}
$$

centered at $\hvbeta$.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Confidence interval for $\mu(\vx)$

Recall

$$
\mu(\vx)=\vx^\top\vbeta
\qquad
\hmu(\vx)=\vx^\top\hvbeta
$$

Since $\hvbeta$ is normal,

$$
\hmu(\vx)
\sim
\Norm\!\left(
\mu(\vx),\;
\sigma^2
\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx
\right)
$$

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Standardized statistic

Replacing $\sigma^2$ by $S^2$ gives

$$
\frac{
\hmu(\vx)-\mu(\vx)
}{
S
\sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
}
\sim t_{n-d}
$$

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval

Therefore

$$
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
t_{n-d,1-\alpha/2}
\;
S
\sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
$$

is a

$$
\class{alert}{(1-\alpha)\text{ confidence interval for }\mu(\vx)}
$$

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Prediction interval for $Y\mid \vx$

## Future observation

For a new observation

$$
Y_{\text{new}}=\vx^\top\vbeta+\varepsilon_{\text{new}}
$$

with

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{new}}\sim\Norm(0,\sigma^2)
$$

independent of the data.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Variance

Then

$$
Y_{\text{new}}-\hmu(\vx)
=
\varepsilon_{\text{new}}
-
\vx^\top(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
$$

so

$$
\var(Y_{\text{new}}-\hmu(\vx))
=
\sigma^2
\left(
1+\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx
\right)
$$

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## $(1-\alpha)$ prediction interval

Therefore

$$
Y_{\text{new}}
\in
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
t_{n-d,1-\alpha/2}
S
\sqrt{
1+\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx
}
$$

which is the

$$
\class{alert}{(1-\alpha)\text{ prediction interval for }Y|\vx}
$$

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Mean vs prediction intervals

Confidence interval for mean response:

$$
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
t_{n-d,1-\alpha/2}
S
\sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
$$

Prediction interval for a new observation:

$$
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
t_{n-d,1-\alpha/2}
S
\sqrt{
1+\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx
}
$$

Key point:

$$
\class{alert}{\text{Prediction intervals are wider}}
$$
